title: Thoughts after 10 years....
pros:
1.  If you love tech, this is a great place.  No doubt you'll talk tech (mostly the MSFT stack) from enterprise to consumer - from PCs to phones to Xboxes - from datacenter to desktop.

2.  What were GREAT benefits are now VERY GOOD (took a small step down) but still probably better than you'll find at 99% of large corporations.  If you've got family - the value of the benefits is even higher.  401k match is nice.

3.  Even with it's struggles MSFT is still a cash printing machine.  This means if you can keep your nose clean and do reasonable work, you can have a stable job, pay your bills, feed your family, and not worry (too much) about layoffs.  The stock you own likely won't tank, but probably won't go up much either.  You'll get a bonus each year and some stock.  It's a decent life if you aren't looking to light the world on fire.
cons:
Brand on Your Resume:  After many years of losing market share and struggling to be at the front end of innovation and the fact that there's 90,000 employees, don't think MSFT is necessarily going to be attractive on your resume to more agile and smaller companies.

Managing Your Career:  Make you say this out loud so it registers - 90,000 employees work there.  Double that for vendors.  It is VERY hard to "stand out" and move up in the company.  Don't expect your manager to be much of an advocate or enabler to help you meet your career goals - they are basically trying to survive the stack rank every year too.  Not familiar with the stack rank?  Check out the 2012 Vanity Fair article called "Microsoft's Lost Decade".
advice_to_management:
I'll type it here - but I don't they are listening...
1.  Help proven talent move laterally in the company for new opportunities.
2.  Kill/evolve the stack rank.  Here's basically how it's viewed:.  30% of the company gets 1s and 2s - and they are happy.  50% get 3s and that basically feels like getting a "C" in school.  This sucks for talented people when a multitude of UNCONTROLLABLE conditions keep you from getting into 1 or 2 range.  The 20% getting anything below a 3 are walking dead.  In summary, 70% of the company walk away from the review cycle feeling like crap.  This is no good.

After 10 years, I'm leaving - it's just a matter of time until I find the right opportunity.  I need to find a company spends 90% of it's time building technology experiences, as opposed to 90% of its time building PowerPoints.  And I expect to refuse the exit interview - because if you really cared what I thought, you wouldn't ask me after I decided to leave.

title: Software Engineer Intern
pros:
Interning at Google's California branch was honestly one of the best decisions I've made early in my career. From day one, the team made me feel like a real contributor rather than just an intern passing through. My mentor was genuinely invested in my growth — always available to answer questions, provide code feedback, and help me navigate the codebase without making me feel like a burden.

The engineering culture here is collaborative in a way that's hard to put into words. People are sharp, but they're also generous with their knowledge. I never felt intimidated asking for help, which made learning so much faster.
cons: Work-life balance can be a challenge. The pace is fast and expectations are high, which is exciting but can feel overwhelming at times. Boundaries are important here — you have to be intentional about logging off.
advice_to_management: Invest in helping interns find balance early. The energy and pace at Google is incredible, but it can be easy to over-extend yourself when you're eager to impress. A brief onboarding conversation around sustainable work habits and setting boundaries would go a long way — especially for interns who are new to the industry and may not yet know how to pace themselves in a high-performance environment.

title: Morale Is Nonexistent & Clique Culture Runs Deep
pros:
•Brand recognition
•Some talented, hardworking individuals (primarily vendors) who genuinely care about delivering results
•Remote work options depending on role
cons:
•Pay has dropped significantly while expectations have increased, including routine unpaid overtime that is treated as the norm rather than the exception.
•Vendors perform the majority of hands-on work, while many FTEs function primarily as managers, reviewers, or overseers rather than contributors. Many FTEs are running full-time businesses while working. Leadership asleep at the wheel.
•Morale is extremely low. Burnout is widespread, and talent is treated as disposable rather than valued. Advancement is dead.
•The workplace is clique-driven and political. Advancement and protection depend more on alignment and relationships than performance.
•Despite public messaging, there is little tolerance for differing viewpoints. If you voice an opinion that doesn’t align with the dominant culture, you risk being sidelined or targeted.
•Poor vendor management: abrupt terminations, lack of notice, no handoff, and zero concern for operational continuity.
•Heavy cost-cutting through external contracting has undermined quality, strained remaining talent, and created instability.
•Quality consulting firms are undercut by low-cost contracting models, resulting in reduced standards and increased pressure on desperate workers navigating layoffs.
advice_to_management:
Open your eyes and engage with what is actually happening on the ground. Attend meetings, listen in on working sessions, and occasionally drop into events and presentations without advance notice. Stop surrounding yourselves with “yes” people and filtered narratives.

Vendors are doing the bulk of the execution. Acknowledge it, compensate fairly, and treat them as strategic partners—not expendable labor—or the organization will continue to erode delivery quality and trust.

Address the normalization of unpaid overtime, rebuild psychological safety, and dismantle clique-driven leadership dynamics. A culture that punishes dissent and exploits overwork will continue to lose its strongest talent.

title: not the old microsoft
pros: Microsoft’s performance review process gives a clear, structured way to talk about impact. It typically encourages you to tie your work to measurable outcomes (delivery, quality, customer value, collaboration), which makes accomplishments easier to explain and helps managers compare contributions consistently across a team. When done well, it also creates a regular rhythm for feedback and growth—so you’re not surprised at year-end—and can help you align on priorities, scope, and career development steps.
cons: The downside is that the experience can feel inconsistent depending on the org, manager, and calibration dynamics. Even with guidelines, expectations may be interpreted differently across teams, and outcomes can sometimes feel influenced by visibility, narrative-writing skill, or shifting priorities rather than purely day-to-day contribution. The process can also add significant overhead (self-assessments, evidence gathering, review writing), and if feedback isn’t frequent during the year, the review may feel more like a paperwork exercise than a genuinely helpful development tool.

title: From Dream Culture to Corporate Greed: A Cautionary Tale
pros:
Strong early culture (at first): When I joined, the culture was collaborative, inspiring, and everything I hoped it would be. For a time, it truly felt like a place where people mattered.

Talented colleagues: I worked alongside some of the smartest, most driven people in the industry. The peer-to-peer experience was the highlight of my time here.

Resources and scale: With record profits and massive market presence, the company has the means to do incredible things—if leadership chose to prioritize people.
cons:
Sudden layoffs despite record profits: In May 2025, the company laid off employees without warning. With a $4T market cap and strong financials, this decision felt unnecessary and deeply misaligned with the values the company once promoted.

Poor leadership decisions: Instead of investing in employees through upskilling or redeployment, executives took the easiest route—cutting jobs. This choice eroded trust and morale across the organization.

Broken rehire process: Despite being fully qualified, having direct manager recommendations, referrals, and submitting 15+ applications, I wasn’t granted a single interview. Meanwhile, similar roles continued to appear on the careers page. This sends a clear message: loyalty and performance are not valued.

Declining culture: Friends who remain at the company describe a toxic, disillusioned environment. The vibrant, people-first culture that once defined this place is gone.

Short-term thinking: The company’s actions reflect a prioritization of optics and profit over people. It’s a stark reminder that corporate greed often outweighs employee well-being.
advice_to_management: Rebuilding trust requires more than slogans and PR. Invest in your people, honor their contributions, and stop treating employees as expendable. Culture is not a marketing line—it’s lived through actions. Right now, those actions speak volumes.

title: This company gets mixed reviews
pros:
Competitive Pay: Compensation is consistently strong compared to industry standards.

Solid Benefits: While not as generous as in the past, benefits remain very good overall.

Exciting Projects: Employees get to work on large-scale, impactful, and often fun projects that shape the tech industry.

Established Reputation: Microsoft is a long-standing, globally recognized company with stability and influence.

Strong Stock Performance: Company stock is highly valued and continues to be a major financial perk.
cons:
Leadership Promotions: Many employees feel that the wrong people are promoted into leadership roles.

Mixed Leadership Culture: Executives often say one thing but expect employees to continue working in the same old, sometimes toxic, Microsoft way.

Stack Ranking: The controversial stack ranking system still exists, creating unhealthy competition.

Opaque Review Process: Performance reviews lack transparency, and often the wrong individuals receive the best ratings.
advice_to_management: Microsoft management should focus on modernizing its people practices by ensuring promotions are merit-based and supported by transparent criteria, aligning leadership actions with stated cultural values to eliminate toxic legacy behaviors, phasing out stack ranking in favor of collaborative and growth-oriented performance frameworks, and making the review process more transparent through clear evaluation metrics, 360-degree feedback, and an appeal mechanism; by addressing these issues directly, the company can strengthen trust, foster healthier collaboration, and retain top talent while continuing to deliver innovation at scale.

title: A Good Place to Start and Grow

The company brand is strong and looks great on a resume.
cons: Work can sometimes feel siloed within large product groups. The promotion process for engineers can be opaque and slow. Bureaucracy can hinder decision-making and slow down projects. Compensation, while good, is often matched or exceeded by competing tech companies. As a new hire, it can be challenging to understand the scope and impact of your projects initially.

title: Strong brand, excellent benefits — but career momentum and bureaucracy can slow you down at Microsoft
pros:
Many employees highlight the robust compensation and benefits package — health care, retirement matching, stock-programs and other perks.

Working at Microsoft means being part of a globally recognized company, often with access to very large scale systems, rich resources, and a technology environment that many find motivating.

The company generally rates well in terms of hiring brand and reputation: on Glassdoor the company averages ~4.0 out of 5 stars in employee reviews.

Many teams offer a fair work-life balance (at least in certain roles/locations), flexibility (remote/hybrid options) and the chance to collaborate with talented colleagues.

Clear possibilities to learn and grow technically (especially if you actively move between teams) as you work on large systems and interesting problems.
cons:
The downside of being a large organisation: many employees report bureaucracy, slow decision-making, many approvals required, and complexity in moving or changing teams.

Career progression can be inconsistent: employees say that your growth often depends heavily on your team, manager, and visibility of your project rather than guaranteed upward mobility.

Workload and expectation vary widely — some roles report long hours or high stress depending on team, customer demands, or project cycle.

Because of the scale and established tech stack, some employees feel there is less freedom to pick technologies, innovate in certain directions or make rapid change.

Some reviews highlight that in non-central locations (outside HQ) or certain teams, visibility, promotion or support may be lower.

title: Great tech, tough transitions — AI pivot
pros:
• Unmatched access to cutting-edge AI tools and platforms. Microsoft’s integration of OpenAI and Azure AI into nearly every product is impressive. If you’re in a technical role, you’ll be exposed to frontier innovation daily.
• Strong compensation for top performers. Stock awards and bonuses are generous, especially if you’re aligned with high-growth areas like Azure or Copilot.
• Leadership is visionary. Satya Nadella’s strategic direction has propelled Microsoft to the forefront of the AI race, and the company’s market cap reflects that.
cons:
• Massive layoffs in 2025 have shaken morale. Over 15,000 roles were cut to fund an $80B AI infrastructure push Windows C... +1. Even tenured employees weren’t spared, and the restructuring felt abrupt and impersonal.
• Performance expectations have intensified. AI usage is now embedded in performance reviews, with mandatory adoption and tracking. This shift favors those already fluent in AI workflows, leaving others scrambling to adapt Windows Central.
• Cultural shifts are real. Traditional sales and marketing roles are being replaced by “solution engineers,” and management layers are being flattened. The pace of change can feel relentless, and internal dissent is not always welcomed opentools.ai.
advice_to_management: Investing in AI is smart, but don’t lose sight of the human element. Transparency, empathy, and career development pathways for displaced or transitioning employees are critical. The company’s future is bright, but the journey there shouldn’t leave loyal contributors behind.

title: Once a great company with purpose — now lost in poor leadership and unrealistic expectations
pros:
Excellent career growth opportunities after recent restructures; employees can move across teams and explore new technical areas.
Strong benefits and education support, including funding for certifications, degrees, and even conferences if you’re a speaker.
Many talented and genuinely kind colleagues who support each other and maintain a collaborative spirit despite constant pressure.
Access to cutting-edge tools and data, which can expand your technical expertise significantly.
cons:
Frequent layoffs have heavily disrupted morale and stability.
A once-respectful culture has shifted toward constant performance comparison, damaging employee confidence and motivation.
Leadership expectations have become unrealistic, assuming that AI and Copilot can replace engineering bandwidth or shorten project timelines drastically.
The engineering execution is currently a major bottleneck; restructuring has slowed delivery and made cross-team collaboration harder.
Work-life balance is almost nonexistent — growth feels tied to sacrificing personal and family time.
Middle management layoffs removed many effective, empathetic leaders, while the core issues in senior leadership remain untouched.
There’s a concerning lack of competitive awareness: while Google is advancing rapidly in threat intelligence and research, Microsoft’s leadership seems indifferent or slow to react.
advice_to_management: Rebuild trust and focus on leadership accountability. The layoffs have removed strong mid-level managers who cared about people and delivery, but the strategic problems come from the top. Listen to employees — not just metrics. Recognize that AI doesn’t fix structural or engineering inefficiencies, and that burnout isn’t a sustainable growth model. If Microsoft wants to lead in cybersecurity and technology again, it needs to prioritize execution quality, not output quantity, and reinvest in people instead of pressure.

title: The deal is no longer worth it
pros: To be honest, I don't have a lot of positives here - see below
cons:
Over 8 years at Microsoft, I was happy to accept that the unstated deal is that MS employees get slower career progression and lower pay than they would at equivalent positions elsewhere, in exchange for a more stable work environment - less risk of layoffs, etc. That deal is out the window at this point - the layoff risk is now as high as anywhere else, but career progression and salary haven't risen to match. The culture has deteriorated as large layoffs have become an ongoing problem, in part because the layoffs frequently feel short-sighted: eg, half of my team was RIF'd in July - including almost everyone who actively wanted to work in the office - shortly before the RTO directive came through. So now they no longer have people who want to work in the office, and instead they're forcing all the people who preferred remote work to start coming in again - many of whom are already looking at exit strategies.

I'd really only recommend a position at Microsoft if you have a short- or medium-term plan to boomerang back out, or if you're looking to get a foot in the door at a big tech or games company.

title: Awful SWE Internship Experience
pros: Good pay and nice location.
cons:
My internship at Microsoft was a terrible experience. My manager, mentor, and skip-level did not care about me or support me. From the very beginning, everything felt disorganized and directionless. The culture is extremely team-dependent, which makes the experience feel like a gamble, and unfortunately I ended up on a team with poor culture, no sense of inclusion, and no guidance.

I never felt welcome throughout the internship, and the lack of support or structure made it hard to succeed or even feel like I belonged. In hindsight, I would have taken another company’s offer without hesitation. The program feels like it has gone downhill, and I cannot recommend interning here.
advice_to_management:
Be intentional about where interns are placed. Managers should genuinely want to host interns, not just take them on as a box to check. My manager was not only unsupportive, but actively harsh, unfair, and dismissive. He created an environment where I felt constantly undermined, unvalued, and set up to fail. No matter how much effort I put in, it was clear I would never receive fair feedback, recognition, or even basic respect. That kind of management makes the internship toxic and demoralizing.

If Microsoft wants interns to succeed, managers need to understand what it means to mentor, investing time, patience, and fairness, and they should be held accountable when they fail to do so. Interns deserve constructive guidance, not negativity and indifference. Just as importantly, place interns on teams with clear business needs and meaningful projects, not groups that might be cut or deprioritized. Otherwise, interns are left gambling with their careers and well-being.

* Senior leadership(CVP level) often impedes progress with decisions that feel disconnected from the realities of product development and customer needs. CVPs and above are disjoint from building and customer feedback process and are difficult and even resistant to involve or inform. This can lead to erratic decision-making canceling projects then months later restarting them as competitors did it, people working on the same thing but in nuanced different ways.
* CVPs really are neglecting areas where the Microsoft brand is strong, in favor of "red ocean" areas where Microsoft could succeed but would face tremendous friction.
* Microsoft is moving to a culture of layoffs where they are not based on performance or any meaningful metric often eliminating people who are incredibly talented devoted and loyal over people who really are stagnant.
* PM leadership continuously fails to define a strategy in any meaningful way to help organize IC teams.
advice_to_management: Actual middle managers (Above GPM/Director) need's a serious refresh... Losing faith in Satya and LT.

title: Nice place to work. Wish they'd be willing to keep good talent onboard.
pros: A highly collaborative team of people that are always willing (if not always able) to help. Supposedly compensation/benefits for permanent employees are pretty great.
cons: The company is quick to terminate employees en masse despite record profits and refuses to replace them. I suspect AI is largely responsible for this (but of course, executives are completely safe from this). I took a contract role with Microsoft and was led to believe there would be a good chance of being brought on full time, but this did not  pan out despite the team losing many good people recently and in the near future. The company's strategy seems to be relying on temporary employees, taking 6 months to onboard them, and then only keeping them around for another year. Microsoft is a perfect example of what "profits over people" looks like.
advice_to_management: Lift the hiring freeze, the company is obviously making more than enough money to do so. Also, don't overly rely on AI, and certainly don't brag about the majority of your OS code being written by AI. It sows distrust among the general population.

title: Not a job—it’s a survival sport
pros:
You might work for one of the most powerful tech companies on Earth, but your clients just want to know:
“How do I reset my Outlook password without calling Steve in IT?”

You're overqualified, underutilized, diplomatically smiling through every meeting, and wielding more buzzwords than actual influence. But hey, at least you’ve got swag, stock options, and an endless supply of acronyms
cons:
Innovation, Meet Regulation
Trying to introduce Power Platform to a local government agency is like trying to install solar panels on a sinking rowboat. Your big goals? Enable collaboration, improve workflows, modernize services.
Reality? Convince the IT director’s cousin not to unplug the on-prem mail server again

You're supposed to “drive adoption” of Microsoft tools... with clients who still pay invoices via fax. You’re constantly pushing for “modern collaboration,” while begging someone to accept a Teams meeting instead of sending a meeting invite from Lotus Notes

When something actually breaks? You get to say your signature line:
“We’ve engaged engineering and will follow up shortly.”
(Translation: “We emailed someone who’s actively ignoring us.”)

You have no decision-making power. You're like a cloud concierge with a laminated menu no one orders from
advice_to_management:
Please continue measuring our impact using vague, feel-good metrics like ‘client sentiment’ and ‘relationship strength.’”
Because if there’s one thing government clients love, it’s being emotionally rated like a Netflix show

We should definitely roll out another internal tool to streamline success planning.”
Nothing says efficiency like having to update the same information in Excel, Power BI, Viva, CRM, OneNote, and Loop—just so leadership can pull a single dashboard no one questions

Let’s never give CSAMs actual authority to make decisions.”
We love being accountable for adoption, renewals, and satisfaction—without budget control, approval rights, or direct engineering access. It's the perfect mix of visibility and helplessness

title: Microsoft
pros:
Interning at Microsoft has been an incredibly enriching experience that truly exceeded my expectations. From day one, I was challenged with meaningful projects that pushed me to grow technically and professionally. The scope and impact of the work made me feel like a real contributor, not just an observer.

One of the most exciting aspects of the internship is the freedom to explore different teams and roles. This flexibility allowed me to discover new areas of interest and learn from diverse perspectives, helping me better understand where I can make the most impact in the tech industry.

The culture at Microsoft is genuinely welcoming and inclusive. I had countless opportunities to get involved—whether through employee resource groups, team events, or networking activities. What really stood out, though, was the accessibility of senior leaders. From casual coffee chats to structured mentorship sessions, I could learn directly from experienced professionals and gain insights into both technical and strategic aspects of the company.

Overall, my Microsoft internship was a perfect blend of challenging work, career exploration, and cultural immersion. It not only strengthened my skills but also inspired me to think bigger about my future in tech. I feel incredibly fortunate to have been part of such a dynamic, innovative, and supportive environment.
cons: I had an amazing internship experience

title: Specialist
pros:
✅ Pros of Working at Microsoft
1. Top-Tier Global Brand

One of the most respected tech companies worldwide.

Extremely strong CV value for FAANG+/Big Tech careers.

2. Strong Work–Life Balance (for Big Tech)

Generally better than consulting firms.

Flexible working hours and hybrid/remote options in many teams.

3. Competitive Compensation & Benefits

High base salary + bonus + stock (RSUs).

Excellent benefits: health insurance, parental leave, learning budget.

4. Cutting-Edge Technology

Direct exposure to:

AI (OpenAI, Copilot, Azure AI)

Cloud (Azure)

Cybersecurity, OS, enterprise software

Real impact at global scale.

5. Career Mobility

Easy to move internally between:

Teams (Azure, AI, Office, Gaming)

Roles (engineering, research, product, architecture)

Countries

6. Engineering-Driven Culture

Strong emphasis on:

Code quality

Design reviews

Documentation

Encourages long-term technical growth.

7. Learning & Research Opportunities

Access to Microsoft Research, internal training platforms.

Strong support for certifications and conferences.
cons:
❌ Cons of Working at Microsoft
1. Slower Pace Compared to Startups

Decision-making can be slow due to scale.

Many layers of approval.

2. Bureaucracy & Processes

Heavy documentation, compliance, and internal tooling.

Can feel restrictive for highly entrepreneurial people.

3. Impact Can Feel Indirect

At a very large company, individual contributions may feel diluted.

Some teams work on incremental improvements rather than breakthroughs.

4. Team Experience Varies A Lot

Culture and workload depend heavily on:

Manager

Org (Azure vs Gaming vs Research)

Same company, very different experiences.

5. High Bar for Entry

Interviews are difficult (system design, coding, behavioral).

Strong competition from top global talent.

6. Stock Growth Can Be Cyclical

RSU value depends on market conditions.

Less explosive upside than early-stage startups.

7. Less Exposure to Business/Client Strategy

Compared to consulting roles, less client-facing and strategic work.
advice_to_management:
🎯 Microsoft Is Best For People Who Want

Long-term technical mastery

Global-scale impact

Stability + innovation balance

AI, cloud, and enterprise leadership roles

Given your interest in AI, brain–computer interfaces, and long-term singularity problems, Microsoft is especially strong if you aim to work on:

AI infrastructure (Azure AI)

Applied research

Human–AI interaction and cognition-related systems

If you want, I can:

Compare Microsoft vs Google vs Amazon

Tailor this for AI / Research / Cloud Architect

Help you answer “Why Microsoft?” for applications or interviews

title: AI obsession has blinded management
pros: A decent salary and benefits, all things considered
cons: The expectation to grow products exponentially, but bureaucracy and lack of autonomy hamper your ability to actually execute on any sort of ambitious vision.
advice_to_management:
The "deal" at Microsoft has always been that our pay targets 70% of FAANG competitors with the promise of being more stable and less cutthroat to retain top talent. Rolling layoffs and stagnating pay have undermined the deal. 

Management is increasingly making shortsighted, nonsensical decisions to squeeze every penny of revenue possible out of customers to free up cash flow to invest in AI. Many of us lowly ICs feel deeply uncomfortable with this AI trajectory being forced upon all of us given the immense damage to the planet as well as the sociological repercussions like mass job loss and misinformation spread. But we're not allowed to (publicly) speak up and advocate for ethical boundaries because Satya is singularly tunnel visioned on AI. 

For those of us in non AI divisions like Xbox, it feels like we've been acquired by a private equity firm and we're being stripped for parts to feed the AI monster. We're going to ruin a perfectly good, profitable 25 year old brand in the process - a brand that means so much to people around the world - all because shareholders have a new shiny obsession.

title: Great
pros: Flexibility (including a decent amount of time so you could work on a Hackathon, although that depends on the team), opportunities to move to other teams (I heard that's not as available right now), great benefits (above many other tech companies), free drinks (Ha!), Hackathons are fun, good salary (although other big tech might be a little higher), flexible vacations (now it's just open without hours, but it depends on the team how flexible that truly is), very flexible on hybrid/remote (although full remote really should be carefully considered, depending on the team, because it could lead to a layoff or something similar), tuition reimbursement (I recommend you take advantage of this and go for a Masters or PHD), onboarding is very organized and cohesive, they know how to scale impact through vendors (so employees can accomplish more), their tools are the most productive (and they all work together, because Microsoft makes them all), and they might have the best book policy (so it's easier to author books and build your reputation more... than it is at other big tech companies).
cons: Salary is a little lower often than other big tech companies, as well as bonuses and shares/stock. It's still good, but often other big tech can be higher (Google, Meta, maybe Amazon or Oracle, depending on the role, etc.). Similarly, promotions are sometimes a little harder to get, and you can't get a promo by transfer to another team (very rare). the review process is a little too time consuming (comparatively, but it's close). Although the Hackathons are nice, it's not really built into the culture much year round (so it might take some effort to do anything effective through that, in finding the right team, etc.). They don't have free food (which is a minimal factor; some big tech do), but their free drinks are nice, and there are plenty of catered events and meetings.
advice_to_management: Stop laying people off! LOL. Also, if you try to get everyone to use AI in order to lay off some of them, then morale drops, nobody likes you, everybody wants to leave, etc. So some of that is taking advantage of the fact that they can't leave and need the job. It's a bad practice.

title: Toxic: Marketing does not care about customers.
pros:
Great benefits - healthcare, Perks+
Employee groups for every interest
Diverse workforce
Lots of activities and networking opportunities on campus
cons:
It is disheartening to see what marketing has become: 
- You exist solely to build PowerPoints for fire drill executive reviews to make your leadership pretend they know what they're doing. 
- Marketers believe their only job is looking good in front of senior leadership. Shiny demos in front of the EVP. Overblown enthusiasm at all hands meetings. Customer centricity - what's that? Huh? Our job is to enable sales?
- "Planning" is not part of leadership's lexicon. 
- Marketing scope is extremely limited - imagine being hired as a PMM and your only job is writing copy for other marketing teams' campaign execution. Actually, you're not the one copywriting. You're providing direction to a vendor who writes it for you, then as the FTE you put on a pretty slide and call it your own. In the end, the end-customer (internal marketing team) refuses to use that messaging you spent months developing because their team did their own messaging testing independently to demonstrate "impact".  
- Most marketing roles encompass writing briefs for agencies who do 98% of the work. 
- Slow career progression, while incompetent, unethical marketing leaders who don't produce results get promoted. 
- Employee Signals is a trap.  Feedback is sought to identify employees who expose leadership issues. Surprised to see your high performing teammate getting PIP-ed? 
- You have to exaggerate and fabricate your contributions at Microsoft to  pass a PMM job interview at another tech company because your scope at Microsoft is so limited. 
- Disingenuous shoutouts to colleagues, disguised as self-congratulatory remarks—all in the name of “collaboration.”
- Who needs actual marketing skills when you can master the far more valuable art of managing 100 (yes, really) marketing stakeholders all “strategically” stirring the same pot?
advice_to_management:
Practice what you preach about being "customer first". 
Hold marketing leaders accountable - they love blaming sales or engineering. 
Reduce reliance on vendors and let the FTEs do some real work. 
Compare PMM job scopes at big tech competitors.

title: Extremely Important Role but people are seen as replaceable
pros: Great benefits.  People take great pride in the work they do.  Microsoft is always at the forefront of technology and innovation.
cons: The role is seen as at the bottom.  Other roles are promoted more frequently and if you haven’t forged good relationships with those you work with, you’ll find their feedback can severely tarnish your year end review.  You can really learn a lot in this role but it can be difficult to have a career doing this. While there are some that have done so, it’s advisable to leverage the learning opportunities to move into other roles as quickly as you can.  This is also one of the only roles that have you on call 24/7.  They’ll tell you that you don’t have to answer the phone at 1am but if something goes awry and you don’t answer, you own the accountability.
advice_to_management: Just be more honest.  People are stack ranked I. Their performance and there is no honest conversation about that.  You’re ability to stay employed in this role has nothing to do with how well you execute for your customers.  It’s more about how you’re doing vs your peers and how you’re contributing to the growth of the organization.
